text: A map is a magnificent act of betrayal — the living, breathing, endlessly unfolding terrain reduced to something you can fold and pocket, territory collapsed into representation until the two become so confused we forget which preceded which. Borges understood this: the perfect map would be the territory itself, which is to say, no map at all. Yet something irreplaceable happens in that violent reduction, some knowledge that only compression makes visible — the way stepping back from a painting reveals what proximity conceals. The cartographer's lines are not lies but a different order of truth, trading the warmth of ground underfoot for the cold clarity of pattern, sacrificing the smell of pine at elevation for the abstract beauty of contour. We navigate, always, by a simplified fiction of where we are.
